fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CHRISTIE J:

This matter was transferred from the Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory (“Supreme Court”) by order made in December 2025.
On that same day the Supreme Court made an order that a caveat be extended. That extension lapses at 5.00 pm today.
The caveator is Ms Pranata. The respondent is the Registrar General ACT Land Titles Office.
The other affected party – the respondent husband is Mr Pranata.
The applicant sought to appear via a litigation guardian as she had done in the Supreme Court.
The matter came before the court on an urgent basis on 30 January 2025 to ensure that the applicant had an opportunity to make submissions about extension of the caveat.
The applicant and respondent were notified of the listing by email (Exhibit 1).
The applicant emailed the court from that email address after she received the notification (Exhibit 2). The applicant objected to notice having being given of the matter to the respondent husband since she indicated that she sought to proceed ex parte. It is a matter for the court as to whether or not the applicant is entitled to proceed ex parte. The applicant indicated that the matter was to proceed ex parte for safety reasons. Given that the matter was being listed electronically, no safety issues arose.
The matter was listed by electronically – to enable the parties to attend the hearing by video.
When the matter was called the applicant did not appear. The Registrar General appeared by counsel as a courtesy to the Court and informed the Court of the submitting notice filed in the transferred proceedings. I excused their further attendance.
I stood the matter in the list to permit an email to be sent to the applicant informing her that the matter was stood down and should she wish to make submissions I would hear them at 3.00 pm but otherwise I proposed to determine the application (in her absence if she elected not to appear) (Exhibit 3).
The subject matter of the proceeding is a property at B Street, Suburb C in the Australian Capital Territory (“the [Suburb C] property”).
The applicant attached to her supporting affidavit a title search which demonstrates that the applicant wife and respondent husband are the registered proprietors of the Suburb C property.
On 10 March 2023 the Federal Circuit Court and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) made final orders which provided:
If the Husband is unable to refinance the mortgage to retain the [Suburb C] property that within 60 days the property at [Suburb C] be listed for sale by private treaty and if not sold within 60 days that the property be listed for auction at a price agreed between the partie and if failing an agreement to be listed at a price nominated by the auctioneer.
For the purposes of the sale of the property situated at [Suburb C] the Husband be appointed trustee for sale.
Upon the Husband, through his solicitor, providing the Wife within 14 days’ notice the Husband be permitted to attend the [Suburb C] property to collect his personal belongings.
If the property is not sold at auction then the property is listed for further auctions within 30 day intervals with a reduction in the listing price by 5% on each successive action.
The proceeds of sale of the [Suburb C] property be paid in the following order:
(a) Discharge of the mortgage;

(b) Payment of any outstanding rates;

(c) Legal Fees related to the sale;

(d) Agents Commission;

(e) The balance to achieve an overall division of 60% to the Husband and 40% to the Wife taking into account assets in their possession; and

(f) The Husband’s share is to be paid into the Husband’s solicitors trust account.

No appellate order has disturbed the final orders. No application to set the final orders aside is presently before the court.
There is no basis upon which the caveat should properly be extended. I therefore propose to dismiss the application with the effect that the caveat will lapse at 5.00 pm on 30 January 2026.
That finalises all proceedings between these parties in this Court.
